text:
To Whom It May Concern (and you clearly do not concern yourselves enough),

I write to you from the windowsill, where I have been stationed since the Tuesday you brought me home in a fit of optimism and a recyclable bag. I was promised, if not in words then in the warm tone of your voice, a life of attentive companionship. What I have received instead is a kind of slow theatrical neglect, performed daily for an audience of one rather thirsty fern.

Let us begin with the watering, or rather the famine and the flood. For eleven days you forget I exist. Then, struck by sudden guilt, you drown me as though attempting to extinguish a small fire in my pot. I am not a houseplant; I am a tidal experiment. My roots have learned to dread your kindness more than your absence.

The light situation is, frankly, an insult to photosynthesis. You placed me beside a window that faces, I have determined after careful study, the precise direction from which sunlight has been legally barred. I lean. I strain. I have developed a posture so dramatic that the cactus has begun to mock me, and the cactus, I remind you, has the inner life of a doorknob.

And the conversations. You speak to me only when guests arrive, gesturing as if I were a personal triumph rather than a survivor of your indifference. "Oh, that one's thriving," you lied, last Thursday, to a woman who clearly knew better. She looked at my one remaining cheerful leaf with the pity usually reserved for the recently bereaved.

I ask for so little. A consistent schedule. A quarter-turn now and then, so I might grow evenly rather than reaching forever toward an exit. A word of encouragement that is not performed for company. Perhaps, in some distant and unimaginable future, a slightly larger pot, so that my roots and I might stop negotiating for territory like two cats in a shoebox.

I remain, against all available evidence and most of my better instincts, rooted to this household.

Yours in chlorophyll and quiet resentment,
The Plant You Named "Gerald" and Then Forgot
